title: Nightjars: Birds Making a Big Comeback
summary: Nightjars are special birds that come out at dusk and dawn. They are making a big comeback in a park called the South Downs National Park. Last year, 78 nightjars were counted, which is double from five years ago. People are helping by taking care of the park's habitat, like keeping dogs on leads and staying on paths. Nightjars fly a long way from Africa each spring and nest on the ground. They have a funny nickname, 'goatsucker,' from an old story that they drank goat milk, but that's not true. The birds are important and need protection because their numbers dropped in the past. Now, with better care, they are doing well again.

background_read: Nightjars are birds that are active at dusk and dawn, known for their churring song. They nest on the ground and migrate long distances from Africa to places like the South Downs National Park in England. In the past, their numbers dropped because of habitat loss, but now conservation efforts are helping them recover. People are working to protect their homes by managing heathlands and encouraging visitors to be careful. This story shows how taking care of nature can help animals thrive again.
Article_Structure: WHO: Nightjars, park rangers, and conservationists
WHAT: Nightjars are increasing in number due to conservation efforts
WHERE: South Downs National Park in England
WHY: To protect the birds and their habitat from disturbance and loss

perspectives.description: The comeback shows that protecting habitats can help endangered species recover.
